author: @MisterkeTube
text: Most often things are made to not last for a single reason: profit.  Why don't led lamps last forever (by design)?  Profit!  Why are caps in power-supplies failing as soon as warranty expires?  Profit!

author: @johnkunze5362
text: Every Tesla will end up in the scrap heap....non-removable batteries. There still are model t's on the road today! Planned obsolescence is filling out garbage dumps. I have some neoprene flap hinges that I installed in the '80's.

author: @hermandormehl
text: The older stuff for me

author: @VeniceInventors
text: Good point about things not made to last not being taken into account as they're not around anymore

author: @aloksrivastava7938
text:
This comment may seem irrelevant to the topic of the video but I recently found out that Toyota has developed a new Water engine. 
This news piqued my interest so I did some research and found: "Scientists Have Developed The Most Efficient Water-Splitting Catalyst."

They say, "The catalyst is made up of a ferrous metaphosphate and a conductive nickel foam platform, a combination of materials the team says is more efficient and less expensive than existing solutions." What's your take on this? Do you think is it really possible or is it just a hoax?

author: @wydinya1211
text: Hello Robert. I watched one of your videos the other day on serpentine coils to make a generator. It brought up a question in my mind that you might be able to answer. If you have two serpentine coils next to each at a slight difference in position. Then if you had segmented magnetic discs on each side of the serpentine coils rotating in different directions, would the magnetic field in the serpentine coils be cancelled out. If so then would it still be the case if the magnets were geared to pass each other at the position of outer and inner parts of the two coils.

author: @OldManSparkplug
text: Thank you for pointing out that of course the artifacts we have from the past were made to last because if they weren't we wouldn't have them!  I've been watching your channel for some time now and I always appreciate your style of teaching.  Science communication is tricky but you never seem to *over* simplify, just enough to make it relatable so your audience understands.

author: @festivetragedy
text: Sorry about Patti. She seems cool

author: @list1726
text: Thank you for posting

author: @CCoburn3
text: Perhaps the biggest difference between, say, the Victorian times and today is the materials we can use to manufacture things. Look at those massive machines from the 1800s. They are massive because they are made of iron. We can make machines that can perform the same job and do it better because we have such a vast range of materials they just didn't have. We can make cheap boxes with flex hinges because we have plastics. They had to use the materials they had available. Because of this, they made fewer things and the more massive stuff tended to last.

author: @DirkLarien
text:
Mr Robert, you might find this interesting. I had a piece of failed PLA print once. And i was just messing with it. Deforming it bending and so on. 
Than it fell into my fresh coffee. 
And what do you know as the deformed material soften in the hot coffee it undid all the deformations essentially healed it. Its shape was almost perfectly back in original shape after it cooled off.

author: @steverichmond7142
text: British Leyland were good at making cars designed not to last.

author: @wimwiddershins
text: A well designed PP live hinge will operate into the 100 thousands of operations.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
Recommend looking at scrapyards, and not working facilities, to figure out what things actually last.  Maintenance, repair, spare parts, and scrap are normally hidden from easy view.  Engineers try to find out how much has been spent on parts and service for existing technology before they go to improve on an old design.
You might not see everything even then -- recently visited the Duesenberg museum.  Lycoming built all their engines over just a couple years of production, then the company spent years using up that single production stock in building new Duesenberg and Auburn cars.  Even for the single run, Lycoming had a huge scrappage rate -- if I remember properly, only 1 in 6 castings passed muster when engine production first started.

author: @TempOne-vh4fd
text: Up date on the flat screen solar panel. turns out you dont have to do all the crazy chemistry stuff to pull charge. You dont even have to take off the polarizer.  all gotta do is strip it down to the screen only and you put the non-polarized down and the smooth glass up  and some AL foil and you get charge.

author: @steve29836
text: Great tutorial as always. You set the hinge to 0.5mm, most people print with a 0.2mm layer height. The slicer will either make it 0.4mm(2 layers) or 0.6mm(3 layers). Personally I design considering layer height so I'd draw it at 0.4mm or 0.6mm thereby knowing exactly how many layers I'm going to get once sliced.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thanks for the info!

author: @Nick_Tag
text:
Rob is the GOAT - Greatest of all Time. 
I just started a PhD in metamaterials for thermo-photovoltaic applications and am constantly taking the approach learnt here -- what can I also make today/ASAP without fancy equipment?
  With part of the lab closed for summer I've realised I could just use kapton tape for my dielectric layer during the interim (!)

author: @scotttovey
text:
I printed off a 170 mm square part and the slicer decided it needed additional edging. I think it is used to prevent warping and reduce the chance of the part coming loose while printing.

After I removed it from the print bed I gave it a close look and concluded that the material which turned out to be a single layer of .23 mm thick, could be used as a protective book cover.

I can't say how long it would last, but it would be an interesting test.

author: @Forensic1Man
text: Robert, It is too bad that people we care for do not last a lifetime! If only we could extend life or restore broken parts easily!

author: @johnwynne-qx6br
text: I can see what you are saying but I do like things that last. And I also understand about cost but it is a false economy saving. I.e buildings to last 40 years that are still being used 60 years later and money spent on a building that had a 40 year lifetime to try and keep  it safe when we know it wasn't designed to last. So a well designed to last building would be cheaper in the long term. Obviously for modeling and prototypes cheap not made to last makes sense. Great video and thought provoking as usual.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there is also the question of inbuilt obsolescence and the non repairable nature of many things we make that are very questionable to my mind

replies.author: @johnwynne-qx6br
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  yes we have all got to make a profit margin but I think for the sake of the planet things need to change. Saying that in the grand scheme of things probably irrelevant, but for human existence quite important. Thankyou for your reply. Great that the channel is back.

replies.author: @Michaelfatman-xo7gv
replies.text: But, by it's very primitiveness, replacement parts that are less complex can let it continue longer.  Think of the difference between a gravel road versus a asphalt road..  Asphalt breaks and heaves, turning into a no go, while one grades a gravel road and she keeps on going.

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: For your novices Dr. Smith, pay attention to the direction of the extrusion when you slice it. You might not want it to follow along the fold. But rather for the extrusion to be perpendicular to the fold.
